concept: Parity of a product: a product is odd only if every factor is odd; if any factor is even, the product is even.
question: Is the product 14 × 9 × 7 even or odd? Justify using parity rules.
solution: 1. Determine parity of each factor: 14 is even (14 = 2 × 7); 9 is odd; 7 is odd. 2. Parity rule: even × odd = even. 3. Apply to first two factors: 14 × 9 is even. 4. Apply rule again: even × odd = even, so (14 × 9) × 7 is even. 5. Therefore 14 × 9 × 7 is even.
paraphrase: First, classify each number by parity: 14 is even, 9 is odd, and 7 is odd. Second, recall the rule that multiplying any even number by any odd number yields an even result. Third, combine 14 and 9: since 14 is even, their product is even. Fourth, multiply that even result by 7: even times odd is still even. Conclusion: the full product 14 × 9 × 7 is even.

concept: Parity of a product: an integer product is odd if and only if every factor is odd; otherwise the product is even.
question: Is the product 13 × 6 × 9 odd or even? Justify using parity rules.
solution: 1. Determine parity of each factor: 13 is odd, 6 is even, 9 is odd. 2. Parity multiplication rule: odd × odd = odd, but even × anything = even. 3. Since 6 is even, the product 13 × 6 × 9 must be even. 4. Therefore 13 × 6 × 9 is even.
paraphrase: First, classify each number: 13 is odd, 6 is even, and 9 is odd. Second, recall the rule that multiplying any integer by an even number produces an even result. Third, because one of the three factors (6) is even, the entire product is guaranteed to be even. Conclusion: 13 × 6 × 9 is even.

question: Is the product 14 × 9 × 7 even or odd? Justify using parity rules.

concept: Parity of a product: odd × odd = odd, odd × even = even, even × even = even.
question: Is the product 13 × 27 × 8 odd or even? Justify using parity multiplication rules.
solution: 1. Find parity of each factor: 13 is odd, 27 is odd, 8 is even. 2. Multiply parities left to right: odd × odd = odd, so 13 × 27 has odd parity. 3. Next: odd × even = even, so (13 × 27) × 8 has even parity. 4. Therefore 13 × 27 × 8 is even.
paraphrase: First, classify each number: 13 is odd, 27 is odd, and 8 is even. Second, combine the first two: the rule odd times odd gives odd means 13 × 27 is odd. Third, combine that result with 8: the rule odd times even gives even means the full product is even. The product 13 × 27 × 8 is therefore even.

concept: Parity of a product: a product is even if at least one factor is even, and odd only if all factors are odd.
question: Is the product 14 × 9 × 7 even or odd? Justify using parity multiplication rules.
solution: 1. Determine the parity of each factor: 14 is even (14 = 2 × 7); 9 is odd; 7 is odd. 2. Parity rule: a product is even if at least one factor is even. 3. Since 14 is even, the entire product is even. 4. Verify: 14 × 9 = 126, and 126 × 7 = 882; 882 is divisible by 2, confirming it is even.
paraphrase: First, classify each number by parity: 14 divides evenly by 2 so it is even; 9 and 7 are both odd. Second, recall the parity multiplication rule: having even one even factor makes the whole product even. Third, because 14 is even, we immediately know 14 × 9 × 7 is even. Fourth, as a check, 14 times 9 is 126, and 126 times 7 is 882, which is divisible by 2, confirming the product is even.
